bonuses.leadin: This opera's overture, which begins with a2 (“ah 2”) trombones playing a forte ascending C minor 9th arpeggio and holding the high D, contains dances named for the spirits of Earth, Water, and Fire. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This opera's overture, which begins with a2 ("ah 2") trombones playing a forte ascending C minor 9th arpeggio and holding the high D, contains dances named for the spirits of Earth, Water, and Fire. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this one-act comic opera by Gustav Holst. This opera has only been performed once since its failed premiere, after which Holst re-arranged the overture as a more popular ballet suite.
Holst’s The Perfect Fool is a parody of this Wagner opera, whose title Arthurian knight is recognized as a “pure fool” by Guernemanz while on a quest for the Holy Grail.

The latest opera Holst wrote before The Perfect Fool tells the story of two lovers from this country who successfully trick Death. In an opera set in this country, a soprano and a mezzo describe a “thick dome” of the title objects in a highly melismatic B major duet.
parts_sanitized: Name this one-act comic opera by Gustav Holst. This opera has only been performed once since its failed premiere, after which Holst re-arranged the overture as a more popular ballet suite.
Holst's The Perfect Fool is a parody of this Wagner opera, whose title Arthurian knight is recognized as a "pure fool" by Guernemanz while on a quest for the Holy Grail.

The latest opera Holst wrote before The Perfect Fool tells the story of two lovers from this country who successfully trick Death. In an opera set in this country, a soprano and a mezzo describe a "thick dome" of the title objects in a highly melismatic B major duet.
answers: India [or Bharat] (The Holst opera is Sāvitri, and the other opera is Léo Delibes’ Lakmé.)
answers_sanitized: India [or Bharat] (The Holst opera is Savitri, and the other opera is Leo Delibes' Lakme.)

bonuses.leadin: In one of this composer’s operas, Nadir and Zurga sing the aria “Au fond du temple saint” while recalling the moment they both fell in love with a mysterious maiden. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this composer of the opera The Pearl Fishers, which is set in India. In another opera of his, the title character sings that “Love is a rebellious bird” in an aria.
In this Bizet opera, the title character sings the Habanera and is eventually stabbed by Don José (“zho-SAY”) in the bullfighting arena.

This French opera by Léo Delibes (“duh-LEEB”), set on the Indian subcontinent, features the Bell Song and the famous Flower Duet. At the end of this opera, the title character kills herself by eating a poisonous datura leaf.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In one of this composer's operas, Nadir and Zurga sing the aria "Au fond du temple saint" while recalling the moment they both fell in love with a mysterious maiden. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this composer of the opera The Pearl Fishers, which is set in India. In another opera of his, the title character sings that "Love is a rebellious bird" in an aria.
In this Bizet opera, the title character sings the Habanera and is eventually stabbed by Don Jose ("zho-SAY") in the bullfighting arena.

This French opera by Leo Delibes ("duh-LEEB"), set on the Indian subcontinent, features the Bell Song and the famous Flower Duet. At the end of this opera, the title character kills herself by eating a poisonous datura leaf.

bonuses.leadin: Name these 19th century opera heroines who commit suicide, for 10 points each:
parts: This singer commits suicide by jumping off a castle parapet at the end of an eponymous Puccini opera.

Having sung the Flowers Duet in the first act, this heroine of a Delibes [duh-LEEB] work eats a poisonous flower in the third act when she realises that the British officer Gérald has a military duty to abandon her.
This character takes poison to avoid submitting herself to the Count di Luna in Verdi’s Il Trovatore.

parts_sanitized: This singer commits suicide by jumping off a castle parapet at the end of an eponymous Puccini opera.

Having sung the Flowers Duet in the first act, this heroine of a Delibes [duh-LEEB] work eats a poisonous flower in the third act when she realises that the British officer Gerald has a military duty to abandon her.
This character takes poison to avoid submitting herself to the Count di Luna in Verdi's Il Trovatore.
